content:
# Joshua Chapter 10 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 233-235 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_010_page_001.jpg`

---

**1** And it was when Adoni-Zedek king of Jerusalem heard that Joshua had captured Ai and had devoted it to destruction — as he had done to Jericho and its king, so he had done to Ai and its king — and that the inhabitants of Gibeon had made peace with Israel and were in their midst,

**2** then they feared greatly, because Gibeon was a great city, like one of the royal cities, and because it was greater than Ai, and all its men were mighty.

**3** And Adoni-Zedek king of Jerusalem sent to Hoham king of Hebron, and to Piram king of Jarmuth, and to Japhia king of Lachish, and to Debir king of Eglon, saying,

**4** "Come up to me and help me, and let us strike Gibeon, for it has made peace with Joshua and with the sons of Israel."

**5** And the five kings of the Amorites gathered and went up — the king of Jerusalem, the king of Hebron, the king of Jarmuth, the king of Lachish, the king of Eglon — they and all their camps, and they encamped against Gibeon and made war against it.

**6** And the men of Gibeon sent to Joshua, to the camp at Gilgal, saying, "Do not let your hand drop from your servants. Come up to us quickly and save us and help us, for all the kings of the Amorites who dwell in the hill country have gathered against us."

**7** And Joshua went up from Gilgal, he and all the people of war with him, and all the mighty men of valor.

**8** And YHWH said to Joshua, "Do not fear them, for into your hand I have given them. Not a man of them shall stand before you."

**9** And Joshua came upon them suddenly — all the night he went up from Gilgal.

**10** And YHWH threw them into confusion before Israel, and he struck them with a great striking at Gibeon, and pursued them by way of the ascent of Beth-Horon, and struck them as far as Azekah and as far as Makkedah.

**11** And it was, as they fled before Israel — they were on the descent of Beth-Horon — and YHWH cast great stones from the heavens upon them, as far as Azekah, and they died. More were those who died by the hailstones than those whom the sons of Israel killed with the sword.

**12** Then Joshua spoke to YHWH on the day YHWH gave the Amorites before the sons of Israel, and he said in the sight of Israel,

"Sun, be still upon Gibeon,
and moon, in the valley of Aijalon."

**13** And the sun was still, and the moon stood, until the nation took vengeance on its enemies.

Is it not written in the Book of the Upright? And the sun stood in the midst of the heavens and did not hasten to go down for about a whole day.

**14** And there was no day like that, before it or after it, when YHWH listened to the voice of a man, for YHWH was fighting for Israel.

**15** And Joshua returned, and all Israel with him, to the camp at Gilgal.

**16** And these five kings fled and hid themselves in the cave at Makkedah.

**17** And it was told to Joshua, saying, "The five kings have been found, hidden in the cave at Makkedah."

**18** And Joshua said, "Roll great stones to the mouth of the cave, and set men over it to guard them.

**19** And as for you, do not stand still. Pursue your enemies and attack their rear. Do not let them enter their cities, for YHWH your God has given them into your hand."

**20** And it was, when Joshua and the sons of Israel had finished striking them with a very great striking until they were consumed — and the survivors who survived of them entered the fortified cities —

**21** then all the people returned to the camp, to Joshua at Makkedah, in peace. No man sharpened his tongue against the sons of Israel.

**22** And Joshua said, "Open the mouth of the cave, and bring out to me these five kings from the cave."

**23** And they did so, and they brought out to him the five kings from the cave: the king of Jerusalem, the king of Hebron, the king of Jarmuth, the king of Lachish, the king of Eglon.

**24** And it was, when they brought out these kings to Joshua, that Joshua called to every man of Israel and said to the commanders of the men of war who had gone with him, "Draw near, put your feet on the necks of these kings." And they drew near and put their feet on their necks.

**25** And Joshua said to them, "Do not fear and do not be dismayed. Be strong and be courageous, for thus YHWH will do to all your enemies against whom you fight."

**26** And Joshua struck them after this and put them to death, and he hung them on five trees. And they were hanging on the trees until the evening.

**27** And it was at the time of the going down of the sun, Joshua commanded and they took them down from the trees and cast them into the cave where they had hidden themselves, and they set great stones against the mouth of the cave — unto this very day.

**28** And Joshua captured Makkedah on that day and struck it with the edge of the sword, and its king he devoted to destruction — them, and every soul that was in it. He left no survivor. And he did to the king of Makkedah as he had done to the king of Jericho.

**29** And Joshua passed on, and all Israel with him, from Makkedah to Libnah, and he made war against Libnah.

**30** And YHWH also gave it into the hand of Israel, and its king, and he struck it with the edge of the sword, and every soul that was in it. He left no survivor in it. And he did to its king as he had done to the king of Jericho.

**31** And Joshua passed on, and all Israel with him, from Libnah to Lachish, and he encamped against it and made war against it.

**32** And YHWH gave Lachish into the hand of Israel, and he captured it on the second day, and he struck it with the edge of the sword, and every soul that was in it, according to all that he had done to Libnah.

**33** Then Horam king of Gezer came up to help Lachish, and Joshua struck him and his people until he had left him no survivor.

**34** And Joshua passed on, and all Israel with him, from Lachish to Eglon, and they encamped against it and made war against it.

**35** And they captured it on that day and struck it with the edge of the sword, and every soul that was in it he devoted to destruction on that day, according to all that he had done to Lachish.

**36** And Joshua went up, and all Israel with him, from Eglon to Hebron, and they made war against it.

**37** And they captured it and struck it with the edge of the sword, and its king, and all its cities, and every soul that was in it. He left no survivor, according to all that he had done to Eglon. And he devoted it to destruction, and every soul that was in it.

**38** And Joshua turned back, and all Israel with him, to Debir, and he made war against it.

**39** And he captured it, and its king, and all its cities, and they struck them with the edge of the sword, and they devoted to destruction every soul that was in it. He left no survivor. As he had done to Hebron, so he did to Debir and to its king, and as he had done to Libnah and to its king.

**40** And Joshua struck all the land: the hill country, and the Negev, and the lowland, and the slopes, and all their kings. He left no survivor. And all that breathed he devoted to destruction, as YHWH the God of Israel had commanded.

**41** And Joshua struck them from Kadesh-Barnea and as far as Gaza, and all the land of Goshen, and as far as Gibeon.

**42** And all these kings and their land Joshua captured at one time, for YHWH the God of Israel was fighting for Israel.

**43** And Joshua returned, and all Israel with him, to the camp at Gilgal.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Textual Basis
- **Primary source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 233-235, as photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman).
- **LXX consultation:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209, folios 168-169), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana. The LXX broadly follows the MT in this chapter but shows notable divergences in the poetic section (vv. 12-13) and in the campaign summaries.

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **v. 1 -- "Adoni-Zedek" (אֲדֹנִי־צֶ֣דֶק):** The name means "my lord is righteousness" or "lord of righteousness." LOGOS transliterates consistently. The LXX renders Αδωνιβεζεκ in some manuscripts, but Vaticanus reads Αδωνιζεδεκ, supporting the MT.

2. **v. 2 -- "they feared greatly" (וַיִּֽירְא֣וּ מְאֹ֔ד):** The subject is plural though Adoni-Zedek was the last named subject. The Hebrew indicates he and his people feared. LOGOS preserves the plural verb as "they feared."

3. **v. 5 -- "camps" (מַחֲנֵיהֶם):** Hebrew *machaneihem*, literally "their camps/encampments," referring to the assembled military forces. LOGOS retains "camps" rather than smoothing to "armies."

4. **v. 10 -- "threw them into confusion" (וַיְהֻמֵּ֣ם):** Hebrew *wayhummem*, from the root ה-מ-מ, a term used for divine panic or confusion (cf. Exod. 14:24, 1 Sam. 7:10). LOGOS preserves this as divine action rather than interpreting as "routed."

5. **v. 11 -- "great stones" (אֲבָנִ֣ים גְּדֹל֡וֹת):** The Hebrew says "great stones from the heavens," then clarifies these as "stones of hail" (אַבְנֵ֣י בָרָ֔ד). LOGOS preserves both terms as they appear in sequence -- "great stones" first, then "hailstones" -- reflecting the Hebrew narrative structure where the second clause interprets the first.

6. **v. 12 -- "Sun, be still" (שֶׁ֣מֶשׁ ׀ דּ֗וֹם):** The imperative *dom* (from דמם) means "be silent, be still, cease." LOGOS renders "be still" to capture the cessation of motion implied by context while remaining faithful to the root meaning. The LXX uses στήτω ("let it stand"), which interprets motion-cessation more explicitly.

7. **v. 13 -- "Book of the Upright" (סֵ֣פֶר הַיָּשָׁ֑ר):** Hebrew *Sefer HaYashar*, a lost Israelite literary collection also referenced in 2 Samuel 1:18. LOGOS translates *yashar* as "Upright" (its literal meaning: straight, upright) rather than the conventional "Jashar," which is merely a transliteration.

8. **v. 13 -- "did not hasten to go down" (לֹא־אָ֥ץ לָב֖וֹא):** Hebrew *lo' 'ats lavo'* -- literally "did not hurry to come/go." LOGOS preserves "hasten to go down" to capture the Hebrew idiom of the sun's passage being delayed rather than fully stopped.

9. **v. 21 -- "No man sharpened his tongue" (לֹא־חָרַ֞ץ...לִשֹׁנ֑וֹ):** Hebrew *lo' charats...leshono* -- literally "no one sharpened/pointed his tongue." This idiom (cf. Exod. 11:7) means no one spoke against or threatened. LOGOS preserves the vivid Hebrew idiom rather than paraphrasing.

10. **v. 24 -- "put your feet on the necks" (שִׂ֣ימוּ אֶת־רַגְלֵיכֶ֔ם עַֽל־צַוְּארֵ֖י):** An ancient Near Eastern act of total subjugation. LOGOS translates directly without explanatory addition.

11. **v. 27 -- "unto this very day" (עַד־עֶ֖צֶם הַיּ֥וֹם הַזֶּֽה):** Hebrew *'ad-'etsem hayom hazeh* -- the word *'etsem* ("bone, substance, very") intensifies "this day." LOGOS renders "this very day" to capture the emphasis. This is the narrator's voice, indicating the stones were still visible at the time of composition.

12. **v. 28 -- "devoted to destruction" (הֶחֱרִים):** The verb *hechrim* (Hiphil of חרם) appears repeatedly in vv. 28, 35, 37, 39, 40 as the campaign summary formula. LOGOS maintains "devoted to destruction" consistently for the *cherem* concept throughout.

13. **vv. 28-39 -- Campaign formula:** The Hebrew uses a repetitive formulaic structure for each city conquered: "passed on...captured...struck with the edge of the sword...every soul...left no survivor." LOGOS preserves this deliberate literary repetition rather than varying the language for stylistic purposes, as the repetition itself carries theological weight.

### YHWH Occurrences
YHWH appears in vv. 8, 10, 11, 12 (x2), 14 (x2), 19, 25, 30, 32, 40, 42 -- thirteen occurrences. LOGOS renders the Tetragrammaton consistently as YHWH throughout. In vv. 40 and 42, the full title "YHWH the God of Israel" (יהוה אֱלֹהֵ֥י יִשְׂרָאֵ֖ל) appears.

### LXX Variants of Note
- **v. 5:** The LXX specifies "five kings of the Jebusites" rather than "Amorites," possibly reflecting a variant tradition about the ethnic identity of the Jerusalem coalition.
- **v. 11:** The LXX adds clarity that the stones were "stones of hail from heaven" (λίθους χαλάζης), combining what the MT presents in two stages.
- **v. 13:** The LXX reads "and the sun stood in the middle of the heaven" (ἔστη ὁ ἥλιος κατὰ μέσον τοῦ οὐρανοῦ), closely matching the MT. However, the Vaticanus text presents a slightly shorter version of the poetic quotation.
- **v. 15:** The LXX Vaticanus omits this verse entirely. This is a significant textual variant; the verse appears to be a premature notice of return to Gilgal (which is repeated in v. 43). Many scholars regard v. 15 as a later harmonizing addition in the MT tradition, and the LXX omission may preserve the earlier text.
- **v. 21:** The LXX renders the idiom about the tongue differently, using a more general expression for "none spoke against."
- **vv. 28-39:** The LXX campaign summaries are generally shorter, sometimes omitting the phrase "and every soul that was in it" or compressing the formulaic language. This may reflect a shorter Vorlage or scribal abbreviation of repetitive material.
- **v. 42:** The LXX adds "at one time" (ἅμα), aligning with the MT's emphasis on the rapidity of Joshua's campaign.
